subject: Tort Law
module_title: Trespass to the Person and Trespass to Land
lesson_title: Battery, Assault, False Imprisonment, Trespass to Land, and Defences

questions.question: The tort of battery is correctly defined as:

options.A: The direct and intentional application of force to another person without their consent
options.B: Any physical contact with another person that causes them actual harm
options.C: An act causing a person to reasonably apprehend an imminent application of force
options.D: Negligent conduct that results in physical contact with another person

questions.explanation: Battery is the direct and intentional application of force to another person without their consent. The act must be direct, intentional (not necessarily intended to cause harm, just the act itself), and without consent. It is actionable per se — no proof of damage is required.

questions.question: In Collins v Wilcock [1984], Goff LJ held that battery may be constituted by:

options.A: Any touching that causes the plaintiff physical pain or injury
options.B: Any physical contact with another person which is not within the limits of what is impliedly consented to by ordinary social interaction
options.C: Only forceful or aggressive physical contact with another person
options.D: Physical contact accompanied by an intention to cause injury to the plaintiff

questions.explanation: Collins v Wilcock [1984] broadly construed 'force' in battery. Goff LJ held that any physical contact not within the limits of what is impliedly consented to by ordinary social interaction may constitute a battery — including touching to attract attention that exceeds the bounds of normal social contact.

questions.question: In Re F (Mental Patient: Sterilisation) [1990], the House of Lords held that medical treatment of a non-consenting patient with a severe mental disability could be lawful if:

options.A: The patient's family members had given consent on their behalf
options.B: The treatment was required urgently and there was no time to obtain any form of consent
options.C: The treatment was in the best interests of the patient — applying the doctrine of necessity
options.D: A court order had been obtained authorising the specific procedure

questions.explanation: Re F (Mental Patient: Sterilisation) [1990] held that medical treatment of a non-consenting incapacitous patient could be lawful if it was in the patient's best interests, applying the doctrine of necessity. This is the key authority for the medical context in battery — consent can be substituted by the best interests standard where capacity is absent.

questions.question: Battery is described as 'actionable per se'. What does this mean?

options.A: The plaintiff must prove they suffered actual physical harm before the claim can proceed
options.B: The defendant is strictly liable regardless of intent
options.C: The claim can only be brought by the plaintiff personally and cannot be transferred
options.D: No proof of actual damage is required — the tort is established once the elements are proved

questions.explanation: Battery is actionable per se — no proof of actual damage is required. Once the elements are established (direct, intentional force, without consent), the tort is made out regardless of whether any harm resulted. The same principle applies to assault and false imprisonment.

questions.question: In battery, the requirement that the act be 'intentional' means:

options.A: The defendant must have intentionally performed the act of contact — not necessarily that they intended to cause harm
options.B: The defendant must have specifically intended to injure the plaintiff
options.C: The defendant must have been aware that the contact would be unwelcome to the plaintiff
options.D: The defendant must have planned and premeditated the contact in advance

questions.explanation: In battery, 'intentional' means the defendant intentionally performed the act — not that they intended to cause harm. If a person intentionally pushes another and that person falls and fractures their wrist, the defendant need not have intended the injury — only the act of pushing.

questions.question: The tort of assault in civil law is defined as:

options.A: The direct and intentional application of force to another person without consent
options.B: An act which causes the plaintiff to reasonably apprehend the imminent infliction of a battery
options.C: Any threatening behaviour, including words alone, that puts a person in fear of harm
options.D: Physical contact accompanied by an intention to cause injury

questions.explanation: Civil assault is an act causing the plaintiff to reasonably apprehend the imminent infliction of a battery. This is distinct from battery (which is the contact itself) and requires (1) an act, (2) reasonable apprehension of imminent battery, and (3) objective reasonableness of the apprehension.

questions.question: In Tuberville v Savage (17th century), the defendant put his hand on his sword and said: 'If it were not assize-time, I would not take such language from you.' The court held there was no assault because:

options.A: Placing a hand on a sword is insufficient to constitute an act for the purposes of assault
options.B: The plaintiff was in a public place and therefore not protected by the tort of assault
options.C: The accompanying words negated any apprehension of imminent battery — the defendant explicitly indicated he would not act
options.D: Conditional threats can never constitute assault under the common law

questions.explanation: Tuberville v Savage is the leading authority on conditional threats. The words accompanying the gesture — indicating the defendant would not act at that time — negated any apprehension of imminent battery. The condition made the threat non-imminent. Words can negate what an action might otherwise threaten.

questions.question: Can words alone, without any accompanying act or gesture, constitute civil assault?

options.A: Yes — especially if the words are sufficiently threatening and put the plaintiff in genuine fear
options.B: Yes — words alone suffice if accompanied by an intention to cause immediate harm
options.C: Yes — under the Civil Liability Act 1961, words alone can constitute assault
options.D: No — assault requires a physical act creating apprehension of imminent battery; words alone, including telephone threats, cannot constitute civil assault

questions.explanation: Words alone cannot constitute civil assault. The element of imminence requires a physical act that creates an apprehension of immediate battery. A telephone threat, however terrifying, is not a civil assault — there is no physical act to create apprehension of immediate physical contact.

questions.question: False imprisonment is defined as:

options.A: The total restraint of a person's freedom of movement without lawful justification
options.B: Any restriction on a person's freedom of movement imposed without their consent
options.C: Physical confinement in an enclosed space without access to food or water
options.D: Detention in a police station or other official facility without charge

questions.explanation: False imprisonment is the total restraint of a person's freedom of movement without lawful justification. The key word is 'total' — it requires a complete restraint of movement, not merely a partial restriction, as established in Bird v Jones [1845].

questions.question: In Bird v Jones [1845], the defendant enclosed part of a public bridge and charged admission to cross it. The plaintiff was prevented from crossing in his chosen direction but was free to turn around and cross by another route. The court held:

options.A: There was false imprisonment because the plaintiff was prevented from proceeding in his chosen direction
options.B: There was no false imprisonment because the restraint was not total — the plaintiff had an available means of egress even though he refused to use it
options.C: There was false imprisonment because the defendant had no right to charge admission to a public bridge
options.D: There was no false imprisonment because the plaintiff had not been physically seized or restrained

questions.explanation: Bird v Jones [1845] established that false imprisonment requires total restraint. The plaintiff had an available means of egress — turning around and crossing by another route — even though he refused to use it. The restraint was not total and there was no false imprisonment.

questions.question: In Meering v Grahame-White Aviation Company [1919], the plaintiff was detained in a room for investigation without knowing he was being detained. The Court of Appeal held that:

options.A: There was no false imprisonment because the plaintiff was unaware of the restraint
options.B: There was false imprisonment only because the detention was for an unlawful purpose
options.C: Knowledge of the imprisonment by the plaintiff is not required — false imprisonment can occur even without the plaintiff's awareness
options.D: The employer was vicariously liable for the employees' conduct in detaining the plaintiff

questions.explanation: Meering v Grahame-White Aviation [1919] established that knowledge of the imprisonment is not required for false imprisonment. The tort is committed once the imprisonment occurs, regardless of whether the plaintiff is aware of it. Lord Atkin observed that a person may be imprisoned even while they sleep.

questions.question: False imprisonment may be justified by lawful authority. Which of the following is the correct principle regarding this justification?

options.A: A defendant's sincere belief that they had authority to detain is sufficient justification
options.B: Any authority — statutory or common law — automatically justifies the detention
options.C: Justification by lawful authority requires that the defendant personally hold an official position
options.D: The authority relied upon must be genuine and strictly complied with — purported reliance on an invalid warrant or exceeding the scope of statutory authority will not justify the imprisonment

questions.explanation: Lawful authority justifying false imprisonment must be genuine and strictly complied with. Purported reliance on an invalid warrant, or detention exceeding the scope of statutory authority, will not justify imprisonment. The lawfulness of the justification is determined by law, not the defendant's subjective belief.

questions.question: Consent as a defence to battery must be:

options.A: Genuine, voluntary, and informed — consent obtained by fraud, misrepresentation, or duress is invalid
options.B: Always in writing to be effective as a defence to battery
options.C: Obtained from a guardian or next of kin if the plaintiff is over 16 years of age
options.D: Expressed verbally and contemporaneously with the act to be effective

questions.explanation: Consent as a defence to battery must be genuine, voluntary, and informed. Consent obtained by fraud or misrepresentation may be invalid. Consent given under duress or compulsion is not genuine consent. And consent to one act does not extend to acts beyond its scope.

questions.question: The defence of self-defence to battery requires that the force used in self-defence be:

options.A: Equal in magnitude to the force threatened by the attacker
options.B: Proportionate to the threat faced — grossly disproportionate force will not be excused
options.C: Used only in response to an actual attack and not in anticipation of one
options.D: Directed only at the attacker and not at any third party

questions.explanation: The key requirement for self-defence as a defence to battery is proportionality. The force used must be proportionate to the threat faced. Grossly disproportionate force will not be excused by the self-defence doctrine, even where some threat was present.

questions.question: The three elements required to establish trespass to land are:

options.A: Actual damage, unlawful entry, and lack of consent
options.B: Intentional damage, physical presence on the land, and absence of a licence
options.C: Direct interference, voluntary act, and interference with land in the possession of the plaintiff
options.D: Continued occupation, refusal to leave when asked, and knowledge that entry is unwelcome

questions.explanation: Trespass to land requires three elements: (1) the interference must be direct — not indirect; (2) the act must be voluntary — not compelled by a third party; and (3) it must be with land in the possession of the plaintiff (who may be the owner or a tenant).

questions.question: Who has the right to sue for trespass to land — the owner of the land or the person in possession?

options.A: Only the legal owner of the land can sue for trespass
options.B: Both the owner and any tenant have concurrent rights to sue for the same trespass
options.C: Only the occupier who has resided on the land for more than one year has standing
options.D: The person in possession — a tenant in lawful occupation can sue for trespass during the tenancy even if they are not the owner

questions.explanation: Trespass to land protects the possessor, not necessarily the owner. A tenant in lawful occupation has the right to sue for trespass during the period of the tenancy. O'Brien v McNamee [1953] illustrates the protection afforded to the possessor of land.

questions.question: O'Brien v McNamee [1953] is the classic Irish authority on trespass to land. What was the nature of the trespass in that case?

options.A: The defendant unlawfully erected a fence on the plaintiff's land — the court awarded an injunction and damages for the direct, voluntary trespass
options.B: The defendant allowed weeds to spread from their land onto the plaintiff's land
options.C: The defendant repeatedly crossed the plaintiff's land without permission
options.D: The defendant's aircraft flew low over the plaintiff's property causing noise and disturbance

questions.explanation: O'Brien v McNamee [1953] is the classic Irish trespass to land case. The defendant unlawfully erected a fence on the plaintiff's land — a direct, voluntary interference with land in the plaintiff's possession. The court awarded an injunction and damages.

questions.question: The Latin maxim 'cuius est solum, eius est usque ad coelum et ad inferos' means, in relation to trespass to land:

options.A: The person who causes harm must compensate the victim — the polluter pays
options.B: Whoever owns the land owns everything up to the heavens and down to the depths
options.C: The owner of land has the right to exclude all persons from their property
options.D: No action arises from a dishonourable cause

questions.explanation: The maxim 'cuius est solum, eius est usque ad coelum et ad inferos' means that the landowner owns everything up to the heavens and down to the depths. However, this has been substantially qualified — Bernstein v Skyviews [1978] limited airspace rights to the height necessary for ordinary use of the land.

questions.question: Bernstein v Skyviews and General Limited [1978] held that a landowner's rights in the airspace above their land extend:

options.A: Unlimitedly upward — any aircraft flying over the land without permission commits trespass
options.B: To 1,000 feet above the land — the boundary set by the Air Navigation and Transport Act 1946
options.C: Only to such height as is necessary for the ordinary use and enjoyment of the land and structures on it — aircraft flying at a reasonable altitude do not commit trespass
options.D: To the height of the tallest structure on the land plus a reasonable buffer zone

questions.explanation: Bernstein v Skyviews [1978] substantially qualified the cuius est solum maxim in relation to airspace. A landowner's rights in airspace extend only to the height necessary for ordinary use and enjoyment of the land and structures. Aircraft flying at a reasonable altitude do not commit trespass — consistent with the Air Navigation and Transport Act 1946 in Ireland.

questions.question: Trespass to land is actionable per se. What is the significance of this for plaintiffs?

options.A: The plaintiff must provide advance notice of the trespass to the defendant before issuing proceedings
options.B: The plaintiff can only claim an injunction — not damages — without proof of harm
options.C: The plaintiff must be the registered owner of the land to bring a claim
options.D: No proof of actual damage is required — the mere act of trespass is sufficient to found a claim

questions.explanation: Trespass to land is actionable per se — no proof of actual damage is required. The mere act of trespass (direct, voluntary interference with land in the plaintiff's possession) is sufficient to found a claim, making it an effective tool even where no physical damage results.

questions.question: A licence as a defence to trespass to land may be:

options.A: Express (explicit verbal or written permission) or implied from the circumstances
options.B: Only express — implied licences are not recognised in Irish law
options.C: Only granted by the legal owner of the land and cannot be granted by a tenant
options.D: Only valid if recorded in a deed or other formal document

questions.explanation: A licence as a defence to trespass to land may be express or implied from the circumstances. Implied licences arise where the occupier has clearly consented to entry by the circumstances — such as a postman entering to deliver mail, or a visitor approaching a front door to knock.

questions.question: A contractual licence to enter land is revoked by the licensor. The licensee refuses to leave. What is their legal position?

options.A: The licensee is entitled to remain for a reasonable period after revocation regardless of whether the licence is contractual
options.B: Once a licence is revoked (and a reasonable time to leave has elapsed), the former licensee becomes a trespasser — though revocation of a contractual licence may give rise to breach of contract
options.C: A contractual licence cannot be revoked without a court order
options.D: The licensee is entitled to remain until the term of the contract expires, regardless of revocation

questions.explanation: A licence may generally be revoked by the licensor. Once revoked, the former licensee must leave within a reasonable time; if they remain, they become a trespasser. However, revocation of a contractual licence may give rise to a breach of contract claim — equity may even restrain such revocation in some circumstances.

questions.question: A persistent trespass — such as a wall erected on a neighbour's land — gives rise to which type of cause of action?

options.A: A single cause of action at the time the wall was first erected
options.B: A claim only available if the plaintiff has suffered quantifiable property damage
options.C: A continuing cause of action — each day the encroachment continues constitutes a fresh trespass
options.D: A claim extinguished after 12 years of adverse possession by the trespasser

questions.explanation: A persistent trespass — such as the erection of a structure on another's land — gives rise to a continuing cause of action. Each day the encroachment continues constitutes a fresh trespass, which has significant implications for limitation periods and the ongoing availability of injunctive relief.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT constitute trespass to land?

options.A: Physically entering and remaining on another's land without permission
options.B: Placing objects on another person's land without their consent
options.C: A licensee remaining on land after their licence has been revoked and a reasonable time to leave has passed
options.D: Allowing weeds to spread from your land onto a neighbour's land

questions.explanation: Allowing weeds to spread from your land onto a neighbour's land is indirect interference — not direct interference. This is better classified as private nuisance, not trespass. Trespass to land requires direct interference. The other options all involve direct interferences.

questions.question: For FE-1 purposes, which of the following best summarises the approach to a trespass to person problem question?

options.A: Identify and apply each tort separately (battery, assault, false imprisonment); apply the elements; note all are actionable per se; and then work through the applicable defences (consent, self-defence, necessity, lawful authority)
options.B: Treat all three trespass torts as interchangeable and apply them jointly to the facts
options.C: Only consider battery — assault and false imprisonment are subsumed within battery for exam purposes
options.D: Apply negligence principles to trespass claims and require proof of damage in all cases

questions.explanation: The FE-1 approach requires identifying and applying each trespass to person tort separately — battery (direct intentional force without consent), assault (reasonable apprehension of imminent battery), and false imprisonment (total restraint without lawful justification). All are actionable per se. Then work through defences: consent, self-defence, necessity, and lawful authority.

questions.question: A surgeon performs an operation on a patient who has signed a consent form for a knee replacement. During surgery, the surgeon also removes a suspicious mole without separate consent. Which of the following is correct?

options.A: The surgeon is not liable — acting in the patient's best interests is always a valid defence to battery
options.B: The surgeon may be liable in battery for removing the mole — consent to one procedure does not extend to additional procedures not covered by the consent form
options.C: The surgeon's additional procedure is justified under the doctrine of necessity in all medical contexts
options.D: The patient must prove actual harm from the mole removal before a battery claim can succeed

questions.explanation: Consent to one act does not extend to acts beyond its scope. A patient who consents to a specific operation does not thereby consent to additional procedures not covered by the consent form. The surgeon who removes the mole without separate consent may be liable in battery — battery is actionable per se, so no harm need be proved.

questions.question: The defence of necessity in trespass to person, as illustrated in Re F [1990], permits:

options.A: Any person to act in a patient's medical interests without consent, provided they have good intentions
options.B: Medical professionals only to treat non-consenting patients, regardless of the patient's capacity
options.C: Treatment of a person incapable of consenting where the treatment is in their best interests and no other justification is available
options.D: Emergency treatment in any circumstance where the patient is physically incapacitated

questions.explanation: The doctrine of necessity in trespass to person, as applied in Re F [1990], permits treatment of a person incapable of giving consent where (1) the treatment is in their best interests and (2) no other justification is available. It does not apply broadly to override the choices of capacitous patients.

questions.question: A garda enters private premises purportedly under a statutory search warrant that has expired. Is the entry trespass to land?

options.A: No — a garda acting in good faith never commits trespass to land
options.B: No — statutory authority always justifies entry by law enforcement officers
options.C: Only if the garda knew the warrant had expired at the time of entry
options.D: Yes — the justification by law defence requires that the authority be genuine and strictly complied with; entry under an expired warrant is not justified

questions.explanation: The justification by law defence requires that the authority relied upon be genuine and strictly complied with. Entry under an expired warrant is not justified by lawful authority — the warrant was invalid at the time of entry. The garda's good faith belief is not sufficient to excuse the trespass.

questions.question: A postman enters a driveway to deliver post. The householder claims trespass to land. Is the postman a trespasser?

options.A: No — the postman has an implied licence to enter the driveway to deliver mail, within the scope of that purpose
options.B: Yes — any entry onto land without express permission constitutes trespass
options.C: Only if the householder had previously given express notice that no entry was permitted
options.D: No — the postman is protected by the constitutional right to freedom of communication

questions.explanation: The postman has an implied licence to enter the driveway to deliver mail. Implied licences arise from the circumstances — it is understood that those approaching a property for purposes such as delivering post have an implied permission to do so within the scope of that limited purpose.

questions.question: A firefighter enters private land to extinguish a fire that threatens neighbouring properties. Is this trespass to land?

options.A: Yes — the entry is without the permission of the landowner and constitutes trespass per se
options.B: Potentially no — the firefighter may rely on the defence of necessity where the entry was to prevent greater harm, provided the necessity was genuine
options.C: No — emergency services are categorically immune from trespass liability under Irish law
options.D: Yes — necessity is not available as a defence to trespass to land

questions.explanation: The defence of necessity may apply to trespass to land where a person enters to prevent greater harm. A firefighter entering private land to extinguish a fire threatening neighbouring properties may rely on necessity — though the defence is narrow and the necessity must be genuine.

questions.question: Which of the following best identifies the distinction between trespass to land and private nuisance?

options.A: Trespass protects landowners; nuisance protects all persons on land including trespassers
options.B: Trespass requires proof of damage; nuisance is actionable per se
options.C: Trespass involves direct interference with land; nuisance involves indirect interference causing unreasonable interference with the use and enjoyment of land
options.D: Trespass is a criminal offence; nuisance is purely a civil wrong

questions.explanation: The key distinction is directness. Trespass to land involves direct interference (physical entry, placing objects, remaining after licence revocation). Private nuisance involves indirect interference — such as allowing weeds to spread, creating noise or smells — that unreasonably interferes with the use and enjoyment of neighbouring land.
